other_observers: Joan M. Collins; I am the original finder and identifier of the reported bird.  Independent Observers:  Cole Wild on 12/28/2006 at approx. 0900 hrs. See attached email from Cole to me.  Cole Wild, Tony Leukering, Lisa Edward, Park
specific_location: 1225 East Douglas Road, Fort Collins, Colorado 80524  NW 1/4 of the NW 1/4 of Section 30 Township 8 North Range 68 West of the 6th PM
description: Robin like in size and shape with shorter tail; bill dark, robin-shaped with upper surface of upper mandible decurved; crown, mantle, upper tail surface all dark gray; under tail mixed black & white with small amount of orange; orange eye stripe above eye originating directly above eye and extending to rear with no stripe forward of eye; breast, sides, & belly orange; flanks generally orange with unpatterned light grey spots transitioning rearward to light grey femoral tract; vent white; black chest stripe and black cheeks; upper wings same dark grey as mantle with orange wing bars; under wings grey with white stripe paralleling anterior and posterior wing edges. This differs from Sibley picture showing single buff stripe on under wing. Please see accompanying documentary photos with birds wings outstretched about to take flight; legs pinkish orange.Behaviors: Bird would enter yard alone, often coming from the east, & land in a mature apple tree with a sunflower seed feeder hanging from a lower branch. Bird would perch for several minutes then drop to the ground beneath the feeder where sunflower seeds cover the snow-covered ground. Bird would feed on sunflower seeds by walking very short distances and rapidly picking up seeds; bird would feed for up to two minutes but was timid and easily disturbed taking flight at the slightest noise or disturbance. Bird would usually wait to drop to ground feeding when few other birds were present; often saw it feeding with several Bluejays, Dark-eyed Juncos, several sparrow species, and several Mourning Doves (I sprinkle millet, ground corn, etc on the ground for the ground-feeding species). If disturbed, bird would return to perch in the apple tree & wait for varying time periods before ground feeding again, or it would fly off to perch in Siberian elms that border the eastern property boundary or nearby old large Plain Cottonwoods, or would leave the property & return at sporadic times.  Bird is always alone. There are numerous mature Russian olive trees on site that attract large numbers of robins & occasional small flocks of Cedar Waxwings; I never observed the subject bird feeding on the olives or joining the robins or Waxwings.Call: Unheard. Always observed from indoors through kitchen window.Plumage: adult
